Estimating Import Lead Times Using Business
Intelligence and Machine Learning Within the
CRISP-DM Framework: A Case Study in
Oil and Gas Services Industry
MOHAMED ANNIS SOUAMES1, LARBI ABDERRAHMANE MOHAMMEDI2,
ISKANDER ZOUAGHI
2, ANGAPPA GUNASEKARAN
3, SAMIA BELDJOUDI2,
AND ABDERRAZAK LAGHOUAG
4
1School of Built Environment, Engineering and Computing, Leeds Beckett University, LS1 3HE Leeds, U.K.
2Department of Industrial Engineering, Ècole Nationale Polytechnique, El Harrach 16200, Algeria

ABSTRACT This study explores the estimation of import lead times in the oil and gas services industry
by combining Business Intelligence (BI) and Machine Learning (ML) within the CRISP-DM (Cross-
Industry Standard Process for Data Mining) framework. Addressing the challenge of limited data availability,
we employ data synthesis techniques alongside machine learning algorithms to model and predict lead
times accurately across diverse import scenarios. The methodology begins with a dual perspective on the
problem, encompassing both business and technical requirements, followed by systematic data collection,
cleansing, and preparation. BI tools facilitate the visualization of key insights, allowing for enhanced data
comprehension and performance tracking. Machine learning models are then applied to predict lead times
under varying import conditions, optimizing decision-making in terms of transport mode, entry port, and
customs handling. The final solution is deployed through an interactive chatbot interface, streamlining access
to lead time estimates for import-export specialists. This integrated approach enables proactive lead time
management, cost optimization, and greater operational efficiency within import processes, providing a
strategic advantage in the industry.
INDEX TERMS Imports, lead time, business intelligence, machine learning, data synthesis, CRISP-DM,
chatbot.
I. INTRODUCTION
The global oil and gas services industry operates within
a complex network of international supply chains, where
timely delivery of critical equipment and resources is cru-
cial for maintaining smooth operations. Companies rely on
efficient logistics to transport specialized machinery and
components to operational bases, often situated in remote
The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and
approving it for publication was Jenny Mahoney.
and challenging locations. The logistical complexity, coupled
with diverse regulatory requirements, customs processes, and
the coordination of multiple stakeholders, makes accurate
import lead time estimation a critical factor for cost manage-
ment and operational reliability. As De Treville et al. [1] and
Ward and Zho [2] emphasized, precise lead time estimation
plays a key role in reducing delays, controlling expenses, and
improving responsiveness to evolving industry demands.
Achieving accurate lead time estimation, however, remains
a considerable challenge. Traditional forecasting approaches

M. A. Souames et al.: Estimating Import Lead Times Using BI and ML Within the CRISP-DM Framework
often fail to adapt to the significant variability within global
import processes, particularly in high-risk sectors like oil and
gas. Chopra and Sodhi [3] underscored that such limitations
are amplified in environments where delays in high-value
shipments result in significant financial impacts. Similarly,
Wang et al. [4] highlighted the need for adaptable models that
can address these uncertainties. Compounding the issue, data
scarcity and inconsistency further hinder accurate forecasts,
particularly in remote regions common to oil and gas opera-
tions [5], [6].
To overcome these challenges, this study integrates Busi-
ness Intelligence (BI) and Machine Learning (ML) within
the CRISP-DM framework to develop a robust, data-driven
model capable of predicting import lead times across varying
logistical scenarios. Machine learning (ML) is commonly
used in data mining to solve various problems. A popular
framework for developing ML applications is the CRISP-
DM [7]. Nodeh et al. [8] demonstrated the effectiveness
of CRISP-DM in managing data mining projects, while
Wahyudi and Arroufu [9] emphasized its value in com-
plex environments. By leveraging the iterative structure of
CRISP-DM, which includes business understanding, data
understanding, data preparation, modeling, evaluation, and
deployment, the study ensures a systematic approach to tack-
ling lead time variability [10], [11].
The central research question addressed in this study
is: How can machine learning and data-driven techniques
improve the accuracy of import lead time estimation in the
oil and gas services industry? To answer this, the study
begins with a comprehensive analysis of business and techni-
cal requirements, followed by data collection, cleaning, and
preparation. Machine learning techniques are then applied
to generate predictive insights, addressing the constraints of
limited and inconsistent data through data synthesis meth-
ods. As Sivarajah et al. [12] pointed out, integrating BI
with machine learning enhances decision-making capabil-
ities. Finally, the solution is deployed via an interactive
chatbot interface, allowing users to obtain real-time lead time
predictions. In a 2018 research by Arunachalam et al. [13],
it was noted that such tools improve operational planning,
offering actionable insights to manage supply chain complex-
ities effectively. This integrated approach delivers a scalable
and reliable solution for the unique challenges faced in the oil
and gas sector.
The implications of this research are multidimensional.
Theoretically, it advances the field of supply chain analytics
by illustrating how machine learning can be applied to predict
lead times in complex, data-constrained environments. Far-
shadfar et al. [14] highlight the value of such approaches for
improving predictive capabilities in dynamic supply chains.
Methodologically, the study demonstrates the CRISP-DM
framework as a structured and practical guide for apply-
ing data science in the oil and gas industry, particularly
when integrating Business Intelligence (BI) and Machine
Learning (ML) for deeper insights [15], [16], [17]. Manage-
rially, this research offers actionable insights for logistics and
import-export managers, enabling proactive decision-making
through data-driven strategies. Additionally, the chatbot inter-
face developed in this study provides a user-friendly tool
for accessing lead time estimates, significantly improving
the efficiency and accuracy of import-related decision-
making [18], [19].
The paper is organized into seven interconnected sections
to provide a comprehensive analysis of lead time estimation
in global supply chains, with a particular focus on the oil and
gas services sector. The first section introduces the study’s
objectives, background, and structure, laying the groundwork
for the subsequent sections. The second section highlights
the pivotal role of lead time estimation in supply chain man-
agement, examining the limitations of traditional forecasting
methods and the potential of BI and ML integration within
the CRISP-DM framework.
The third section presents the research model, outlining
the use of BI, ML, and the CRISP-DM methodology in
supply chain analytics while detailing the specific machine
learning models applied in this study. The fourth section
describes the methodology, including the rationale for select-
ing CRISP-DM as the guiding framework, the unique chal-
lenges posed by the oil and gas industry, and the technical
steps involved in data preparation, model development, and
deployment.
The fifth section focuses on the case study, offering an
overview of the industry’s supply chain organization and
the critical import processes that drive lead time estima-
tion. The sixth section details the implementation of the
CRISP-DM framework, explaining each phase from business
understanding to deployment and presenting the resulting
model performance and findings. The seventh section dis-
cusses the study’s broader implications, providing theoretical,
methodological, and managerial contributions while reflect-
ing on its significance for supply chain operations. Finally,
the paper concludes by summarizing the key contributions,
acknowledging limitations, and suggesting directions for
future research to further refine and generalize the proposed
approach.
II. LITERATURE REVIEW
Effective lead time estimation is crucial for global sup-
ply chain efficiency, particularly in the gas & oil industry,
where logistical challenges are complicated due to legal
requirements and lengthy processes that span different remote
locations. Recent progress in data analytics allows better visi-
bility across the different supply chain stages, while the rapid
development of machine learning techniques has given rise to
a new set of techniques for estimating lead times in logistics
and supply chains and hence allowing better optimization at
a micro and macro level.
This section provides a literature review of the topic and is
divided into three parts: the first part will analyse the impact
of lead time estimation in global supply chains, the second
part provides a review of the related works that integrated
business intelligence (BI) and machine learning techniques

into supply chain optimization. Finally, the third part will
address the use of CRISP-DM in literature as a data science
framework applied for the specific use-case of supply chains.
A. THE CRITICAL ROLE OF LEAD TIME ESTIMATION AND
LIMITATIONS OF TRADITIONAL APPROACHES IN
GLOBAL SUPPLY CHAINS
Effective lead time management is critical for optimizing
operations and maintaining competitiveness in global supply
chains, particularly in high-stakes industries like oil and gas
services. The prominence of lead time reduction gained trac-
tion with the rise of Just-in-Time (JIT) production systems
in the 1950s, as discussed by De Treville et al. [1]. Compa-
nies in this sector navigate extensive, multinational supply
chains that often involve remote and challenging locations,
where accurate lead time estimation becomes essential for
aligning logistics with project timelines, managing inven-
tory, and ensuring service quality. Precise predictions allow
firms to allocate resources efficiently while mitigating risks
associated with disruptions such as regulatory delays or
transport interruptions. Recent studies emphasize that accu-
rate lead time estimation enhances operational efficiency
and cost management, particularly in complex supply chains
[20], [21].
However, achieving accurate lead time predictions is inher-
ently challenging due to the variability and complexity of
global logistics. In the oil and gas sector, shipments fre-
quently involve specialized equipment that undergoes strict
regulatory scrutiny, leading to unpredictable customs delays
and clearance issues. Weijermars [22] and Basiri et al. [23]
observe that logistical bottlenecks, infrastructure disparities,
and varying documentation requirements across regions fur-
ther complicate the estimation process. These factors render
traditional deterministic models inadequate, as they fail to
address the dynamic and variable nature of modern supply
chains. Farahani et al. [24], and Escaith et al. [25] similarly
note that lead time variability in international trade often
stems from external influences, such as customs regulations
and diverse transportation modes, which are difficult to cap-
ture with conventional forecasting techniques.
Traditional methods for lead time estimation, including
regression analysis, time-series forecasting, and linear mod-
els, have been widely adopted for their simplicity and ease
of implementation. These approaches rely on analyzing his-
torical data to identify patterns, to extrapolate future lead
times. While effective in stable environments, these models
struggle to perform in industries like oil and gas, where unpre-
dictability and variability are the norms. Framinan [26] and
Ivanov [27] argue that deterministic models, though practical
under controlled conditions, often fail in dynamic supply
chain environments that frequently deviate from historical
trends.
Moreover, traditional statistical models lack the flexi-
bility to respond to sudden disruptions, such as customs
hold-ups, route changes, or unexpected infrastructure delays.
Enarsson [28] highlights that their reliance on static his-
torical data limits adaptability, making them unsuitable for
addressing new scenarios or unique regulatory challenges.
To overcome these shortcomings, researchers have explored
hybrid models that combine deterministic methods with
stochastic or simulation-based approaches. For example,
Nikolopoulou and Ierapetritou [29] shows that hybrid mod-
els can model the probability distributions of lead times to
account for uncertainty. However, these models often fall
short of capturing the dynamic, interdependent interactions
within global supply chains.
Yang and Shami [30] further argue that while hybrid
models improve adaptability through automated processes
such as model selection and tuning, they remain limited in
handling concept drift and variability, particularly in IoT-
driven environments. Similarly, Sansana et al. [31] highlight
that even robust hybrid models, which integrate mechanis-
tic and data-driven frameworks, cannot match the real-time
learning capabilities offered by advanced machine learning
algorithms. In dynamic industrial settings, machine learning
algorithms excel at adapting to disruptions, providing real-
time predictions, and addressing the variability inherent in
modern supply chains, making them a promising alternative
for lead time estimation. Benlahreche et al. [82] also empha-
size that integrating BPM with IoT can enhance real-time
decision-making, ensuring more accurate predictions and
improved operational efficiency in dynamic environments.
B. INTEGRATING BUSINESS INTELLIGENCE AND
MACHINE LEARNING IN LEAD TIME ESTIMATION
FOR THE OIL AND GAS SERVICES INDUSTRY
Business Intelligence (BI) and Machine Learning (ML)
are driving transformative changes in supply chain analyt-
ics, enabling organizations to leverage data-driven insights
for improved operational efficiency and decision-making.
BI offers stakeholders real-time monitoring and visualiza-
tion of critical performance metrics, including lead times,
shipment statuses, and inventory levels. By converting raw
data into actionable insights, BI tools provide transparency
and control, essential for identifying and addressing bot-
tlenecks within supply chains. Managing risks in supply
chains, particularly in complex sectors like oil and gas,
requires a structured approach to prioritize and mitigate
threats. Business Performance Analytics (BPA), as an exten-
sion of Business Intelligence (BI), plays a vital role in
identifying performance variables, uncovering risks, and
revealing interdependencies within operations. Raffoni et al.
[32] emphasized that effective BPA implementation requires
addressing data quality, organizational competencies, and
cultural shifts. Hatami-Marbini et al. [83] further developed
a DEA-driven risk management framework that helps orga-
nizations prioritize risks, such as criminality and terrorist
attacks, in the Nigerian oil supply chain. This integrated
approach enhances decision-making and optimizes lead time
prediction by addressing key challenges in risk management.

In the oil and gas services industry, where logistical
operations are often complex due to specialized equip-
ment and long-distance transportation, BI has emerged as
a valuable tool for optimizing performance. Kocaoglu [33]
highlights how BI tools empower companies in this sec-
tor to improve visibility and streamline operations, even
in challenging logistics environments. By integrating BPA
principles, organizations can further enhance their ability
to diagnose performance issues holistically, encouraging
cross-functional dialogue and proactive problem-solving.
While BI focuses on monitoring and visualization,
ML introduces advanced predictive capabilities. By analyz-
ing historical and real-time data, ML models can uncover
hidden, non-linear patterns, enabling companies to antici-
pate disruptions and delays. Mohammadpoor and Torabi [34]
emphasized that ML allows organizations to shift from static
decision-making to adaptive strategies, particularly in indus-
tries where lead time variability depends on external factors
like customs clearance processes, regulatory requirements,
and transportation availability. Eissa [35] further observed
that integrating ML within supply chain analytics signifi-
cantly enhances operational resilience, making it possible to
adapt to unexpected changes in dynamic logistics environ-
ments. In the oil and gas sector, this predictive capacity is
critical, as delays in equipment shipments can have cascading
effects on costs and timelines Al-Rbeawi [36]. Furthermore,
as Bordeleau et al. [37] noted, the successful adoption of BI
and analytics in organizations requires more than just techno-
logical resources; factors such as organizational learning and
culture significantly influence the creation of business value.
This perspective underscores the need for a holistic approach
to BI and analytics integration, particularly in complex supply
chains where adaptability and organizational alignment are
critical for success.
With the rapid development of information technology,
global competition has intensified, and the future of sup-
ply chain management (SCM) is poised for significant
transformation, particularly with the integration of artifi-
cial intelligence (AI). Helo and Hao [38] highlighted that
AI-driven approaches are reshaping key SCM domains such
as planning, scheduling, optimization, and transportation.
Their research underscores the value of AI-based business
models in generating actionable insights and creating new
opportunities for supply chain innovation. Fosso Wamba et al.
[39] further emphasized the growing importance of AI appli-
cations in operations and SCM, noting their potential to offer
practical benefits and to bridge gaps between industry prac-
titioners and scholars. They called attention to the need for
more research and dialogue to explore AI’s transformative
potential in this field.
Against this backdrop, machine learning techniques tai-
lored to supply chain challenges have proven particularly
effective for lead time prediction. Decision trees, for instance,
are widely used for their interpretability, as they help
identify significant variables such as transport delays and
customs processing times. However, as Nodeh et al. [8] and
Kapavarapu et al. [40] pointed out, decision trees tend to
overfit in complex datasets. Random forests address this issue
by averaging results across multiple decision trees, improving
accuracy and effectively handling high-dimensional data in
dynamic supply chains. Meanwhile, gradient boosting algo-
rithms like XGBoost iteratively refine predictions by learning
from prior errors, which makes them suitable for complex,
non-linear relationships often encountered in supply chain
processes [41]. Jin et al. [42] and Akbari and Do [43]
demonstrated the effectiveness of Support Vector Machines
(SVMs) in managing both linear and non-linear dependen-
cies within interrelated variables, making them particularly
relevant for predicting logistical metrics like lead times. Neu-
ral networks, although computationally intensive, provide a
promising avenue for future applications when richer, large-
scale datasets are available [44].
The synergy between BI and ML is increasingly seen as
essential for addressing the challenges of modern supply
chains. While BI tools provide the necessary visibility for
monitoring KPIs and identifying disruptions, ML models
enhance this capability by predicting delays and enabling
organizations to act proactively. Kocaoglu [33] demonstrated
that integrating BI dashboards with ML-driven predictive
models has significantly improved lead time estimation, min-
imized delays, and optimized costs in the oil and gas industry.
For instance, real-time analysis of shipment statuses, cus-
toms processes, and transport conditions allows companies
to anticipate bottlenecks and take corrective measures before
issues escalate.
Moreover, advanced enterprise systems such as Enterprise
Resource Planning (ERP), Warehouse Management Systems
(WMS), and Transportation Management Systems (TMS)
further amplify the impact of BI and ML integration. Ste-
fanovic et al. [45] emphasized how these systems offer a
unified platform for data integration and operational con-
trol, enhancing transparency and enabling scenario-based
planning. Zhu et al. [46] discussed the role of emerging
technologies, including Digital Twins and Supply Chain
Control Towers, in providing advanced analytics capabili-
ties that align with the demands of global supply chains.
Zouaghi and Laghouag [84] further highlighted how align-
ing key success factors (KSFs) with ERP implementation
strategies can optimize system integration, thereby improv-
ing lead time estimation accuracy and overall supply chain
performance.
The convergence of BI and ML represents a shift from reac-
tive to proactive supply chain management. By improving
lead time prediction, enhancing operational efficiency, and
building resilience, this integration has proven particularly
valuable in high-stakes industries like oil and gas. Moving
forward, continued development and adoption of advanced
analytics frameworks will enable companies to navigate the
complexities of global logistics, ensuring adaptability, cost
efficiency, and long-term competitiveness.

C. APPLYING THE CRISP-DM FRAMEWORK TO INTEGRATE
BI AND ML IN SUPPLY CHAIN DATA SCIENCE PROJECTS
The CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data
Mining) framework offers a structured and iterative approach
for managing data science projects, comprising six phases:
Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Prepara-
tion, Modeling, Evaluation, and Deployment. Its widespread
adoption in supply chain analytics is largely due to its abil-
ity to ensure alignment between technical implementation
and business objectives, particularly in projects that inte-
grate machine learning (ML) and Business Intelligence (BI).
As Bokrantz et al. [47] and Harno et al. [48] emphasize,
CRISP-DM is especially valuable for complex industries,
where clear alignment at every phase of data analysis is
essential for delivering strategic and operational value.
A notable application of CRISP-DM is its use in supporting
strategic supply chain segmentation decisions, as highlighted
by Kharlamov et al. [49]. Their research demonstrated how
a CRISP-DM-based framework could help a multinational
company transition from a single make-to-order strategy to a
segmented supply chain strategy. By mining operational data,
they identified key segmentation criteria, such as demand
volume, demand variability, and delivery frequency, which
enabled the development of tailored strategies for each seg-
ment. This example underscores CRISP-DM’s capability to
integrate data mining, BI, and ML to support strategic supply
chain objectives.
Further demonstrating CRISP-DM’s versatility, Jacob-
sen and Tan [50] explored its application in food safety
management within a global fast-food franchise. By lever-
aging sensor-based monitoring systems and mining datasets
containing over 4,000 alarm entries, they developed a trans-
ferable association rule model. This scalable model provided
actionable insights and reliable visual information, enabling
multiple managers to monitor their respective quality perfor-
mance effectively. Their study highlights how CRISP-DM
can contribute to digital transformation, offering empiri-
cal insights into the integration of data-driven systems for
improved operational monitoring and decision-making.
One of the key strengths of CRISP-DM lies in its capacity
to harmonize data mining (DM), BI, and ML across the
analytics lifecycle. During the data understanding and prepa-
ration phases, DM techniques identify patterns and structure
unorganized datasets, laying the groundwork for ML algo-
rithms. These algorithms are then applied in the modeling
phase to address specific supply chain challenges, such as
lead time estimation or inventory optimization. BI tools play
a complementary role during the evaluation and deploy-
ment phases by translating analytical outputs into actionable
insights aligned with organizational goals. Bohanec et al.
[51] note that this synergy creates analytics solutions that
are both strategically relevant and operationally effective—
critical in supply chains where decision-making must balance
technological feasibility and managerial priorities.
The flexibility and comprehensiveness of CRISP-DM
make it particularly suitable for integrating BI and ML into
supply chain management. For instance, Gbededo et al. [52]
demonstrated how structured methodologies like CRISP-DM
help managers identify inefficiencies and address chal-
lenges in high-complexity environments. In the present study,
CRISP-DM serves as the backbone for lead time estimation
within the oil and gas sector. By leveraging data synthesis
techniques to overcome data scarcity and employing ML
models for prediction accuracy, this framework provides a
practical solution for managing logistical complexities [8].
Similarly, Hamdani et al. [53] illustrated the iterative benefits
of CRISP-DM in a logistics optimization project, showcasing
its ability to refine predictive models continuously and navi-
gate the socio-technical challenges associated with Big Data
Analytics (BDA) implementation.
The work of Bohanec et al. [51] further validates the
utility of CRISP-DM in integrating ML models with explana-
tory frameworks, facilitating organizational learning and
decision-making. By employing action design research
(ADR), their framework bridges the gap between technical
models and user acceptance, ensuring that predictive analyt-
ics aligns with business needs. Their findings highlight the
potential of CRISP-DM to enhance transparency and trust in
ML-based solutions, a critical factor in high-stakes industries
such as oil and gas.
In the current research, several machine learning mod-
els, decision trees, random forests, gradient boosted trees
(GBT), support vector machines (SVMs), and linear regres-
sion, were evaluated for lead time prediction. These models
were assessed using performance metrics such as mean abso-
lute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE),
with cross-validation ensuring their robustness and relia-
bility. Amirian et al. [54] and Gharbi et al. [55] observed
that integrating these models with BI dashboards provides
logistics managers with intuitive, real-time platforms for
decision-making, improving operational efficiency. Kumar
and Sharma [85] further highlighted how machine learning
models, such as random forests, can significantly enhance
supply chain risk prediction, offering improved accuracy in
forecasting lead times and mitigating potential disruptions,
thus optimizing overall supply chain performance in the oil
and gas industry.
Nevertheless, existing research reveals gaps in the inte-
gration of BI, ML, and CRISP-DM specifically for lead
time estimation in the oil and gas sector. Most studies have
focused on BI or ML independently, without offering a uni-
fied methodology for their application. Addressing this gap,
the present study introduces a structured CRISP-DM-based
framework that combines BI and ML to meet the sector’s
unique logistical and regulatory challenges. Nodeh et al. [8]
stress the importance of CRISP-DM as a standard for achiev-
ing high-quality data analysis and predictive accuracy across
projects.

By combining BI, ML, and CRISP-DM, this research
develops a cohesive model that addresses the complex-
ity of lead time estimation. BI enables data aggregation,
visualization, and reporting, while ML enhances predic-
tive capabilities, enabling logistics managers to proactively
address potential disruptions. CRISP-DM ensures that these
processes are organized into a clear methodology, with each
phase aligning seamlessly with business objectives. Ham-
dani et al. [53] and Gbededo et al. [52] illustrate how
such a structured approach enables actionable, data-driven
decision-making.
The deployment of these solutions through user-friendly
tools, such as chatbots and BI dashboards, further enhances
their practical value by providing real-time insights to
decision-makers. This study highlights the benefits of inte-
grating structured methodologies with advanced analytics
to tackle complex supply chain challenges. By bridging
theoretical frameworks with practical applications, this com-
prehensive approach offers a robust model that can be applied
not only in the oil and gas sector but also across industries
grappling with similar logistical complexities.
III. METHODOLOGY
This study applies a structured and systematic approach to
address the complexity of lead time estimation in the oil
and gas services industry. The chosen methodology, CRISP-
DM, provides a clear framework to guide each phase of data
science and machine learning model development, ensuring
that each step aligns with business objectives and opera-
tional needs. This section explains the rationale for selecting
the CRISP-DM methodology, details the case selection pro-
cess, addresses data challenges, and describes other critical
methodological considerations.
A. METHODOLOGY SELECTION: CRISP-DM FRAMEWORK
There exist several frameworks that outline the steps for
a proper study that relies on data and modeling similar to
this paper. Some of these frameworks include: CRISP-DM,
SEMMA and KDD, to name a few. CRISP-DM deals with
the full cycle of data and modeling in a business context,
it provides a sound business understanding. SEMMA is a
framework focused on technical data manipulation and mod-
eling while KDD is most suitable for extracting knowledge
from structured data sources such as databases.
In the context of this study, The CRISP-DM framework
was selected as it aligns data science techniques and machine
learning modeling with business objectives and processes,
an important aspect when studying complex supply chains.
This business understanding and alignment is lacking in other
frameworks such as SEMMA and KDD.
CRISP-DM has a structured iterative process composed
of six phases, Business Understanding, Data Understanding,
Data Preparation, Modeling, Evaluation, and Deployment,
create a systematic pathway that progresses from problem
definition to practical implementation. Wirth and Hipp [10]
highlighted its effectiveness in managing complex projects,
while Martínez-Plumed et al. [56] emphasized its adaptability
to dynamic industries like oil and gas, where evolving project
requirements demand ongoing model refinement to improve
predictive accuracy.
Each phase of CRISP-DM is designed to ensure both
technical precision and practical applicability of machine
learning models. For instance, the Business Understanding
phase focuses on aligning lead time estimation objectives
with broader organizational goals. Following this, the Data
Understanding and Preparation phases address critical data
quality challenges, ensuring reliable inputs for the modeling
process. Schröer et al. [11] noted the importance of this
phase in delivering trustworthy results. The Evaluation phase
provides a rigorous assessment of model performance, facili-
tating improvements where necessary, while the Deployment
phase ensures that the predictive models are accessible to
stakeholders. As Schröer et al. [57] discuss, this accessibility
is essential for enhancing the model’s operational value and
ensuring its strategic alignment with business processes.
This
structured
methodology
seamlessly
integrates
CRISP-DM with machine learning and BI, creating a robust
approach for lead time estimation in the oil and gas services
industry. By delivering actionable insights, it enhances oper-
ational efficiency and decision-making in complex supply
chain environments.
Figure 1 illustrates the iterative nature of the CRISP-DM
methodology applied in this study. The circular design under-
scores the continuous feedback loop between its six phases,
with data positioned at the core of the process [58]. This
central role of data ensures that the methodology remains
aligned with business goals and operational needs, empha-
sizing adaptability and ongoing refinement in lead time
estimation workflows within the oil and gas sector.
B. CASE STUDY: OIL AND GAS SERVICES
The oil and gas services industry was selected for this study
due to its uniquely intricate supply chain and critical reliance
on accurate lead time estimation. Delays in importing special-
ized equipment to remote or challenging locations can result
in significant financial and operational repercussions. Unlike
many other sectors, the oil and gas industry faces heightened
complexities, including stringent regulatory requirements,
extensive transportation routes, and the need to coordinate
multiple stakeholders across diverse regions. Dempster et al.
[59] emphasized the substantial logistical hurdles posed by
these factors.
This challenging environment provides an ideal context
for exploring the capabilities of machine learning (ML) and
Business Intelligence (BI) in addressing operational ineffi-
ciencies. By leveraging the CRISP-DM framework, this study
demonstrates the potential of ML-driven models to enhance
lead time predictability and operational efficiency. Further-
more, the insights gained from this case study are not limited
to oil and gas; as Goeken and Börner [60] suggested, similar
supply chain challenges exist in high-stakes industries like

In the data cleaning and transformation phase, issues
such as missing values, outliers, and inconsistencies were
resolved through standardized procedures. Variables were
normalized, gaps were imputed, and scaling was applied to
prevent any single variable from disproportionately influ-
encing the model results [63]. To further enhance predictive
power, feature engineering techniques were used to derive
new variables—such as shipment weight or transport route
complexity—while correlation analysis and feature impor-
tance ranking identified the most relevant predictors. These
approaches, discussed by Rane et al. [64], ensured that the
models captured critical relationships within the data.
To achieve an optimal balance between accuracy, flexibil-
ity, and interpretability, a variety of machine learning models
were explored. Linear regression, praised by Chudik et al.
[65] for its simplicity and ability to capture linear trends,
served as a baseline for identifying initial patterns. For
more complex relationships, decision trees were employed
due to their interpretability, as they segment data into log-
ical branches based on feature distributions. Nodeh et al.
[8] and Vairagade et al. [66] further noted that random
forests, by combining multiple decision trees, mitigate over-
fitting and enhance accuracy through ensemble learning. For
non-linear relationships, gradient boosting models such as
XGBoost were particularly effective, with Zhong et al. [67]
TABLE 2. Main lead times for the import-export processes.
highlighting their strength in minimizing prediction errors,
making them well-suited for dynamic supply chain forecast-
ing tasks. Support Vector Machines (SVMs), recognized for
their ability to model intricate decision boundaries, were also
tested. However, Akbari and Do [43] and Li [68] acknowl-
edged the computational challenges of SVMs, particularly
when dealing with large datasets.
The performance of all models was rigorously evaluated
using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Squared
Error (RMSE) as key metrics. To ensure model robustness
and generalizability, cross-validation techniques were applied
across multiple test scenarios, as advocated by Lim et al.
[69]. This comprehensive evaluation ensured that the selected

2) IMPORT COST ESTIMATION
Having estimated the various lead times related to the import
process, we now shift our focus to estimating the associated
costs.
Storage costs are incurred when goods are held in customs
warehouses. Estimating this cost is straightforward since the
daily unit cost for each warehouse is already known. Using
the estimated release lead time, the total storage cost is cal-
culated using the formula:
Storage Cost = Daily Unit CostWarehouse ∗Release LT (3)
Transport costs are associated with moving goods from
entry points to operational bases, a process managed by
customs brokers. These costs are specified in contracts and
depend on three key parameters: Entry Point, Destination
Base, and Shipment Weight, which determines the type of
vehicle required.
Broker handling costs are straightforward to determine as
they are explicitly stated in the customs broker’s contract.
However, customs clearance costs are challenging to estimate
due to their variability and dependence on customs regula-
tions.
The total import cost is expressed as:
Ctotal =Ctransport(Xi) + Cstorage(Yj) + Cbroker(Zk) + Ccustoms
(4)
where:
• Xi : Chosen vehicle for transport
• Yj : Entry point (Airport/Port)
• Zk : customs broker assigned for clearance
E. SOLUTION DEPLOYMENT
The deployment phase is the final stage in the CRISP-DM
methodology, aiming to integrate the developed models into
an operational environment for consistent use. This step
ensures that the predictive models can function seamlessly
in production without requiring frequent retraining or adjust-
ments.
For deployment, the trained models are serialized into
Pickle files (.pkl) using PyCaret. These files facilitate easy
integration into other systems, as they can be reloaded with-
out re-training. The selected best-performing models for each
lead time are 3 Support Vector Machine (SVM) models for:
CFD, Declaration, Transport Lead Time and Linear Regres-
sion model for Release Lead Time. Each model is saved
as a separate Pickle file. For Transport Lead Time, a future
enhancement could involve using Google Maps APIs or sim-
ilar services for an initial estimate, which could then be
fine-tuned by a simpler predictive model. These serialized
files can be deployed on cloud platforms like AWS (Amazon
Web Services) or Microsoft Azure using containerization ser-
vices such as AWS ECS or Azure Container Apps. However,
due to data confidentiality constraints, local deployment was
selected as a viable solution until discussions with IT and
information systems teams are finalized.
With the models exported in a usable format (pickle for-
mat), a complete end-to-end pipeline was developed. This
pipeline enables users to input shipment details, perform nec-
essary transformations, and calculate estimated lead times.
It also provides scenarios for minimizing delays and costs.
The process begins with users entering shipment details
such as weight, business line, legal entity, quantity, and
other relevant attributes. Users can also specify comparison
dimensions, such as Mode of Transport (MOT), Customs
Broker (CCA), or Port of Entry, and select multiple dimen-
sions simultaneously for comparison. Based on the selected
comparison dimensions, the system generates k instances
of shipment data, where k represents the number of values
for the chosen dimensions. For example, if the user wishes
to compare lead times across three modes of transport, Sea
Freight, Air Freight, and Land Freight, three data instances
are created. These instances are then passed through the
trained models, and the estimated lead times for each scenario
are displayed. The workflow of this system is depicted in

Furthermore, our study underscores the value of hybrid
data generation methods, combining Tabular Variational
Autoencoder (TVAE) neural networks with discrete-event
simulation. This approach addresses data limitations in sparse
environments, aligning with recent theoretical advancements
in the field [75], [76]. Zouaghi and Rouibi [87] further
emphasize the importance of managing information dis-
tortion in supply chains, which adds another layer of
complexity to lead time prediction. This hybrid approach
enhances predictive accuracy in complex, data-limited
environments.
B. METHODOLOGICAL IMPLICATIONS
Methodologically, this study demonstrates the effective-
ness of combining machine learning with data augmenta-
tion techniques to enhance model performance, particularly
in environments constrained by limited data availability.
By leveraging the Tabular Variational Autoencoder (TVAE)
as a synthetic data generator, we successfully expanded
the dataset while preserving the original data’s patterns
and relationships. This approach significantly improved
prediction reliability, supporting the growing consensus
that well-generated synthetic data can mitigate overfit-
ting and enhance the generalizability of machine learning
models [77].
Furthermore, the integration of discrete-event simulation
provided a process-specific understanding of lead times.
This method allowed for the identification and modeling of
bottlenecks and delay patterns within the logistics process,
aligning with findings from simulation-based studies in sup-
ply chain management that emphasize its value for capturing
process-level dynamics and variability [78].
The deployment of an interactive chatbot further highlights
the practical application of technological tools to simplify
complex machine learning outputs. By using Streamlit and
Python, we developed a user-friendly interface that deliv-
ers real-time predictive insights, ensuring accessibility for
end-users without technical expertise. This innovation aligns
with recent advancements in supply chain digitalization,
where intuitive real-time interfaces facilitate swift scenario
analysis and enhance decision-making efficiency [79]. This
methodological approach not only bridges the gap between
technical complexity and operational usability but also under-
scores the role of digital tools in modernizing supply chain
analytics.
C. STRATEGIC IMPLICATIONS
Model-driven decision-making offers significant strategic
advantages in optimizing shipment scheduling. By leveraging
predictive models, organizations can better forecast poten-
tial delays and optimize shipping routes, resulting in more
accurate scheduling and timely deliveries. This ability to
predict disruptions in advance enables proactive adjustments,
reducing the risk of delays and improving overall operational
performance.
In addition, integrating machine learning models into
decision-making
processes
allows
for
effective
cost
optimization. Predictive insights help identify the most
cost-effective
shipping
routes
and
minimize
expenses
such as storage fees and demurrage costs. By accurately
predicting lead times and assessing transport options,
companies can avoid inefficiencies and enhance budget
control.
Furthermore, model-driven approaches are critical for
selecting the right customs broker. By assessing brokers’
past performance, machine learning models can identify the
most reliable partners and predict the likelihood of cus-
toms delays, ensuring timely and compliant shipments. This
reduces the risks associated with customs disruptions, safe-
guarding smooth operations.
The adaptability of model-driven decision-making also
plays a key role in enhancing supply chain agility. Real-time
data and continuous model refinement allow organizations
to quickly respond to changes, such as regulatory shifts or
unexpected disruptions, ensuring that operations remain on
track.
Overall, model-driven decision-making enhances strategic
decision-making by providing transparency and data-driven
insights. This enables logistics managers to align decisions
with broader business goals, optimizing resources, reducing
lead times, and improving customer satisfaction, all of which
contribute to a competitive advantage.
D. MANAGERIAL IMPLICATIONS
From a managerial perspective, this predictive system
delivers significant operational advantages by enabling
import-export specialists to make data-informed decisions
based on scenario-driven lead time and cost estimations.
These insights provide a strategic foundation for optimizing
shipment scheduling, selecting efficient customs brokers, and
determining the most reliable modes of transport, key factors
for meeting internal KPIs and maintaining project timelines.
The integration of model-driven decision-making highlights
the strategic value of data analytics in streamlining logistics
workflows, supporting previous findings that data-driven sys-
tems can substantially reduce lead times and associated costs
in global supply chains [80].
Moreover, the system enhances managerial oversight by
quantifying performance variations across multiple dimen-
sions, such as customs broker efficiency, transport mode
reliability, and port-specific delays. This level of granu-
larity equips managers with actionable insights to identify
and address process inefficiencies, allocate resources more
effectively, and negotiate performance-based contracts with
logistics partners. By integrating performance-based con-
tracts with logistics partners, organizations can leverage
system-generated insights to enhance accountability and
service quality. These contracts, tied to key performance
metrics such as on-time delivery and customs efficiency,
encourage logistics partners to optimize their operations.

The data-driven approach reduces costs, mitigates risks
by identifying potential disruptions early, and fosters con-
tinuous improvement. This strategic alignment enhances
collaboration, ensuring that both parties work toward the
same goal of improving overall supply chain performance.
The chatbot interface, which facilitates scenario comparisons,
further empowers managers to dynamically adjust logistics
plans using real-time analytics, aligning with Li’s observa-
tions [68] on agile supply chain management practices.
The system’s scalable architecture, designed for potential
deployment on cloud platforms like AWS or Microsoft Azure,
reinforces its enterprise-wide applicability. This ensures that
other divisions within the organization can replicate and
benefit from similar predictive capabilities. As global sup-
ply chains face growing uncertainties, the system supports
resilience-focused strategies, enabling organizations to adapt
quickly to evolving trade conditions. Such flexibility is criti-
cal for enhancing supply chain robustness [72]. This approach
not only drives operational efficiency but also strengthens the
organization’s capacity to respond proactively to disruptions
in global logistics.
VI. CONCLUSION
This study demonstrates the transformative potential of
machine learning and synthetic data generation for enhanc-
ing predictive accuracy in lead time and cost estimation
within the import process. By implementing Support Vector
Machines (SVM), Linear Regression, and Tabular Variational
Autoencoders (TVAE), we successfully reduced forecasting
errors and provided import-export specialists with actionable
insights. The integration of these advanced analytics models
with an interactive chatbot interface has proven effective
in streamlining decision-making, facilitating scenario-based
analysis, and empowering logistics teams with data-driven
insights for optimizing resources. This approach highlights
the importance of predictive analytics in refining supply chain
management processes, particularly for time-sensitive and
cost-intensive operations.
A. SYNTHESIS
The contributions of this study are primarily threefold. First,
it demonstrates the power of machine learning in enhancing
operational efficiency within logistics by accurately forecast-
ing lead times and associated costs. By leveraging models
trained on both real and synthetically augmented data, our
approach enables organizations to better anticipate poten-
tial delays and strategically manage their resources. Second,
the use of TVAE and discrete-event simulation illustrates
an effective approach to overcoming data limitations often
encountered in complex supply chain environments. These
methods allow for the generation of high-quality synthetic
data, providing a scalable solution for situations where histor-
ical data alone may be insufficient. Lastly, the development
of a chatbot interface enhances accessibility and usability,
translating complex analytics outputs into actionable insights
directly usable by logistics personnel. This approach aligns
with the growing emphasis on real-time, user-friendly sys-
tems in supply chain analytics, bridging the gap between
technical outputs and practical applications.
B. LIMITATIONS
Despite the significant findings, this study does have several
limitations. One key limitation is the dataset’s time scope,
restricted to the year 2022, which may not capture seasonal
or year-to-year variations that impact import lead times.
Additionally, while TVAE-generated data provides realistic
augmentation, it may still lack some nuances inherent in real-
world data, especially for highly variable customs clearance
costs and unexpected regulatory changes. The study also
relies on domain-specific constraints coded manually, which,
while effective for this use case, may limit scalability across
different products or regions. Lastly, while cloud deployment
on platforms like AWS or Azure could improve scalability
and accessibility, privacy and security concerns prevented a
full-scale cloud deployment in this study, which could limit
access for distributed teams and require local infrastructure.
C. FUTURE PERSPECTIVES
Building on the foundations of this work, future research
could aim to further expand the dataset to capture multi-year
trends and seasonal patterns, enhancing model accuracy and
resilience. Integrating real-time data sources, such as GPS
tracking, could refine transport lead time predictions and
offer more responsive, dynamic insights. Exploring alter-
native synthetic data generation techniques like CTGAN
could provide an even more comprehensive data augmenta-
tion approach, improving model diversity and handling more
complex variable relationships.
To enhance the system’s accessibility and scalability,
future efforts could focus on cloud-based deployment with
robust security protocols, allowing enterprise-wide access
and supporting distributed logistics teams. Additionally, the
implementation of adaptive learning could enable continuous
model updates with incoming data, improving the system’s
ability to adjust to evolving conditions in the logistics envi-
ronment. Finally, integrating reinforcement learning could
empower the system to simulate and select optimal logistics
strategies, enhancing its ability to proactively minimize lead
times and costs.
In the same vein, to improve import lead time performance,
a combined method proposed by Yen et al. [81] could be
applied through integrating strategic, tactical, and operational
decision-making into the data mining process, optimizing
each phase to align with business objectives.
In conclusion, this study sets a foundation for predic-
tive analytics in import logistics, showcasing how machine
learning and synthetic data can drive efficiency and strategic
advantages. By continuing to develop and expand this frame-
work, organizations can enhance supply chain resilience,
agility, and responsiveness to meet the dynamic demands of
global trade.
